event: The Road to Emmaus — the risen Christ walking with two disciples
symbols: three pilgrims walking together — the risen Christ joining His disciples unrecognized on the road
walking staffs — the pilgrim journey through life where Christ walks alongside unknown
putti in the tree — angelic witnesses to the hidden presence of the risen Lord
ruins in the background — the old order passing away as the new resurrection reality breaks in
description: A painting depicting three men walking together along a road through a landscape with trees and distant ruins. The central figure, identified as the risen Christ, wears a blue robe and gestures with one hand as He speaks. He has a calm, authoritative presence. The two companions, dressed as pilgrims with walking staffs and simpler garments, walk on either side, listening intently. Above them in the tree, three winged putti (cherubs) observe the scene, with one gesturing. The landscape includes rocky terrain, a large tree, and architectural ruins in the distance.
theological_description: This painting depicts one of the most theologically rich post-Resurrection narratives, recorded in Luke 24:13-35. On the day of Resurrection, two disciples were walking to Emmaus, discussing the events of Christ's death, when the risen Jesus Himself drew near and walked with them — but they were kept from recognizing Him. As they walked, Jesus 'beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, explained to them what was said in all the Scriptures concerning Himself' (Luke 24:27). This is the definitive Christian hermeneutic: all of Scripture points to Christ. The disciples' inability to recognize Jesus reveals that the risen body, while real, exists in a new mode that transcends ordinary perception. Their hearts 'burned within them' as He opened the Scriptures (Luke 24:32), demonstrating that the encounter with the risen Christ happens primarily through the Word. They would only recognize Him later in the breaking of bread (Luke 24:30-31), connecting Emmaus to the Eucharist — Christ known in Word and Sacrament. The Emmaus road became the paradigm for all Christian life: walking with Christ who is present but often unrecognized until He opens eyes of faith.

scripture_refs.reason: Two disciples walking to Emmaus joined by the risen Christ who walks with them unrecognized
Jesus explaining all the Scriptures concerning Himself — the central action depicted as He gestures while speaking
Christ recognized in the breaking of bread — the climax of the Emmaus journey
Did not our hearts burn within us as He opened the Scriptures — the disciples' inner transformation
